title: The Pool of Bethesda
subtitle: Christ finds the man who lay sick thirty-eight years

stillness: Many lay here, waiting, year upon year.
Wait now, with them, for the stirring of the water.
gospel: After this there was a feast-day of the Jews,
and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.
Now there is at Jerusalem, by the temple, a pool,
which in Hebrew is called Bethsaida, the house of the flocks,
In these lay a great multitude of the sick,
of the blind, the lame, the withered,
waiting for the moving of the water.
For an angel of the Lord descended at a certain time into the pool,
and he that went down first into the pool after the moving of the water
was made whole of whatsoever infirmity he lay under.
And there was a certain man there,
that had been eight and thirty years in his infirmity.
Him when Jesus had seen lying, He saith to him:
behold: There was at Jerusalem, beside the temple, a pool of the flocks,
into which the rain-waters dripping down from the temple were gathered.
It is called the house of the flocks,
because there the bodies of the beasts lay in the porch,
until they were washed by the ministers.
It had five porches, and openings round about,
that through many places the ministers might go down to the water.
In these porches lay, in little cells and resting-places,
a great multitude of the languishing,
the blind, the lame, the withered, and others sick,
waiting for the moving of the water.
And there lay a certain man, a paralytic, upon his pallet,
having thirty and eight years in his infirmity.
consider: See here the patience of the sick man,
who waited so many years to be delivered, and did not give up;
and by this he appeared the more worthy to be healed.
For he had fallen into this infirmity because of a sin going before,
and had long been punished;
and because he had borne the penalty patiently,
it was fitting that help be brought to him.
The Lord Jesus saw him, not only with the eye of the body,
but with the eye of mercy,
and said to him: Wilt thou be made whole?
As if He said: It is in thy own will;
for thou, by sin, wast to thyself the cause of thy infirmity.
Turn then thy will away from sin, and toward Me,
as toward the Author of salvation, and thou shalt be cured.
He does not ask as one doubting of the will,
but to lift him up into the hope of health,
of which already he had as it were despaired,
and to kindle the more his desire.
Many were sick there, yet one was made whole;
for one Lord, one faith, one baptism.
Woe then to those who hate unity!
prayer: heal me, languishing, worn down by a long and evil sickness,
and paralytic, weighed down by a lasting numbness toward the good.
Grant me that I may rise, withdrawing from sin,
and take up my pallet, bearing the burden of penance for it,
and walk, going forward in the good,
from good to better, from virtue to virtue.
And being made whole, by Thy gift,
may I strive to abstain from sins henceforth,
lest something worse befall me, here or in time to come.
Grant me also, good Jesus, that following Thee in Thy humility,
I may despise human and earthly glory,
and not desire to be praised or lifted above others,
but seek the glory of Thee alone,
and long to please Thee alone. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Propter quod Dominus Jesus cum hunc non solum oculo corporis, sed etiam miserationis vidisset, dicit ei: Vis sanus fieri?
latinJewel.english: And so the Lord Jesus, when He had seen this man not only with the eye of the body but with the eye of mercy, said to him: Wilt thou be made whole?
